name: Asset Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy outlines the safeguarding measures for RRS’s intellectual property
(IP) and assets. It ensures that everyone at RRS:
1. Protects RRS’s intellectual property rights
2. Respects others’ intellectual property rights
3. Follows all relevant laws around intellectual property
4. Safeguards, identifies, tracks, and manages RRS’s assets
This policy applies to both {{ current configuration.organization_short_name }}’s
and others’ IP, as well as all technology assets, including systems, hardware, and
data sets directly used in business operations. It also applies to all employees
within RRS that use or access company assets.
Protecting RRS’s Intellectual Assets and Safeguarding
Measures
To protect RRS’s intellectual property and assets, we:
Classify and categorize assets based on their importance and risk levels
Limit access to assets to only those employees whose job duties require it
Assign a clearly defined owner to each asset in the Carbide platform
Ensure all employees are responsible for the care, custody, and security of
the assets assigned to them
Follow the Endpoint Hardening Policy and all other prescribed policies
Valuing Assets
Assets will be assigned a value that reflects their importance to RRS. This value
will inform the level of protection required and help manage risk more
effectively.
Respecting Others’ Intellectual Property
In addition to protecting RRS’s intellectual property and assets, it is essential
and required by law to protect the intellectual property of other parties. At RRS,
we:
Identify all relevant regional, national, and international requirements
relating to intellectual property, copyright, patents, and data flow
Make sure all users follow these requirements regarding intellectual
property rights
Maintaining the IP and Asset Inventory
The Security Officer is responsible for intellectual property and asset
management and must review the inventory at least annually.
This review involves:
Confirming whether the asset is still being used
Ensuring the IP and asset is being used correctly under the license
Reviewing who has access to the IP and assets and whether the access is
appropriate
Additionally, there will be continuous monitoring and reviewing of the
effectiveness of the measures taken to protect the assets. This will ensure that
assets are always protected, and asset-related risks are managed effectively.
Implementing Security Measures
Based on its classification, specific security measures and countermeasures will
be developed and implemented for each asset to protect it from unauthorized
access, theft, loss, damage, or destruction.
Adopting a Change Management Procedure for
Intellectual Property and Assets
If someone at RRS wants to either introduce new IP or assets or change the use
of existing IP or assets, the person responsible for IP and assets must:
Review the intellectual property or asset that is being added or changed
Document the intellectual property or asset and add it to the inventory of
IP and assets
Approve the IP or asset for use within RRS

name: Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery (BCDR)
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy outlines the requirements RRS has set to ensure the least downtime
possible to services and systems provided to customers and that support
critical business functions.
This policy is for all critical system owners, delegated administrators, and
individuals assigned backup responsibilities or roles.
BCDR Plan and Backup Guidelines
The BCDR plan consists of concurrent activities, plans, policies, and procedures
included in keeping services and systems up and running that support the
operations of the business throughout their lifecycle, whereas backup
guidelines ensure the recovery of data, devices, and systems in the event of
unexpected interruptions or failures.
Automated and Secure Backups
Backups should be run via automated processes and tools where possible and
must be protected by being physically and logically secured and encrypted.
Access must be limited to key administrators. Backup logs are checked regularly
to ensure appropriate access and backup performance.
Backup Readiness
All backups should be kept in two separate locations to ensure data availability
in case of a single-point failure. Backups may be retained in physical or within
cloud instances. In some cases, management may entrust a service provider to
manage backups in its own way.
Backup Testing and Secure Backup Storage
Backup testing of all critical data stores should be performed at least quarterly
to ensure backups are working correctly and that recovery procedures are
functional and current. Furthermore, all backups must be securely stored to
protect and maintain business continuity.
Alternate Processing Capabilities
As part of the BCDR plan, the organization must have alternate processing
capabilities in place to allow continued business operations during an
interruption or outage of its primary computing infrastructure. This includes
having redundant systems and the ability to shift workloads to alternate
processing environments.
Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery Testing
BCDR plans are documented for each of RRS’s core business functions and
systems that support them. These plans are reviewed, revised, and tested
annually when leadership changes, and significant organizational changes occur.

name: Cloud and Network Security
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy governs the management of RRS’s cloud hosting environment
where it hosts its web application. This policy must be followed by the
individuals assigned to managing or accessing this environment, excluding
those who only access via RRS’s online application interface.
RRS stores substantial amounts of confidential information within the Cloud
environment. Confidentiality, integrity, and availability of this information and
the applications hosted within it are critical to RRS, its customers, employees,
and stakeholders.
Third-party Cloud Services Shared Responsibility
Model
RRS may utilize cloud services from third-party providers to support its
operations. RRS and the third party are collectively responsible for the security
of the leased environment. The shared responsibility model defines which
party is responsible for which elements. The responsibilities and obligations of
RRS are outlined in the agreement. RRS’s Security Team is responsible for
ensuring both the third party and RRS’s administrators follow the guidelines.
Access Control
Access to the hosting environment is restricted to a small group of trained,
vetted, and trusted individuals. The Identity and Access Management Policy
governs access to cloud environments. It is the responsibility of the cloud
environment administrators and RRS’s Security Team to ensure that the cloud
environments comply with the Identity and Access Management Policy. See the
Identity and Access Management Policy for additional information.
Configuration Management
RRS uses the AWS/Azure/GCP security baseline. This baseline is managed using
AWS Security Hub/Azure Security Center/GCP Security Command Center,
which is integrated with Carbide to ensure that misconfigurations or other
vulnerabilities are tended to in an expedited manner.
Network Security Management
Network settings must be configured, and management must be in place by
RRS including the following:
A network diagram must be maintained and updated annually, and any time
there is a significant change to the private cloud network. A significant
change is any change that causes the information in the current network
diagram to become inaccurate.
Network firewalls must be in place to protect RRS private cloud network
from external threats.
Outbound traffic filtering must be configured on network firewalls
protecting RRS networks to prevent systems within the private cloud
network from calling out to malicious services and domains.
Everyone authorized to access the private cloud network must be
identified and documented. A list of authorized users must be maintained.
If remote access is required to the private cloud network, all remote
administrative access to these systems must be encrypted using
technologies such as Virtual Private Networks (VPNs).
Admins accessing the private cloud network must have MFA enforced on
each access occurrence.
General user access to database and application configurations must be
monitored and restricted.
Admin user access to database and application configurations must be
monitored and controlled.
Network Firewall
A network firewall must be in place in the cloud environment. The network
firewall must be configured to do the following:
Network Firewalls must have secure, encrypted administrative access. All
management access must be limited to only approved networks to have
administrative access.
Outbound filtering of all outbound connections must be configured on the
network firewall to prevent unauthorized access and malicious services.
Limiting the types of outbound traffic will reduce the risks of rootkits,
viruses, and malware if a host of endpoints is compromised.
RRS will only permit outbound traffic from recognized services.
Network Firewalls should be reviewed to monitor all services and
applications installed on the device. No unnecessary services or
applications should be enabled on network firewalls.
Only devices following the Endpoint Hardening Policy should be used to
admin access network firewalls.
Clock services on network firewalls must be synchronized with RRS’s other
networking hardware using NTP or another approved synchronized service.
Having synchronized clock services across the organization’s vital systems
will help during incident response, backup restorations, and disaster
recovery scenarios.
All network rules applied to the network firewall and network must be
reviewed annually and approved by the Security Team. Each rule should be
audited to ensure that it is necessary and still required for RRS’s network. If
the rule can be simplified, it should be updated accordingly.
All network firewall and router rules must be reviewed at least annually.
Audits must cover each rule, what it is for, if it is still necessary, and if it can
be improved.
The Security Team must document any changes to network firewall rules.
By default, the network firewall must include a “deny all” rule and a rule to
prevent connections to the network firewall itself.
All dropped or rejected packets must be logged by the network firewall.
Network Time Protocol (NTP)
NTP is a protocol used to synchronize system clock times in a network. All RRS
systems in the cloud environment must be configured to use centralized NTP
servers to maintain time synchronization.
All time servers must remain synced to International Atomic Time.
At least one NTP server must be configured to request time updates.
The cloud service provider provides time syncing via NTP.
Access to time data must be restricted to only personnel with a business
need to access this data.
Security Systems
RRS must ensure that appropriate security systems are in place and sufficient
to meet the organization’s risk appetite and compliance requirements. Systems
in place and managed by RRS include the following:
Amazon Web Services
Identity and Access Management: AWS Identity & Access Management
(IAM)
Detection: AWS Security Hub, Amazon Inspector, Amazon GuardDuty
Infrastructure Protection: AWS WAF, AWS Shield
Data Protection: Amazon Macie
Secrets Management: AWS Key Management Service (KMS)
Incident Response: Amazon Detective
Azure
Identity and Access Management: Azure RBAC, Azure AD
Detection: Azure Security Center
Infrastructure Protection: Azure Security Center, Application Gateway
Data Protection: Azure Information Protection
Secrets Management: Azure Key Vault
Incident Response: Azure Security Center
Google Cloud Platform
Identity and Access Management: Identity and Access Management
Detection: Google Cloud Security Command Center
Infrastructure Protection: Web App and API Protection (WAAP), Google
Cloud Firewalls
Data Protection: Cloud Data Loss Prevention
Secrets Management: Cloud Key Management
Incident Response: Chronicle
Logging and Monitoring
Logging
All information systems and network components must generate logs
of relevant events, including access attempts, system changes, and
security incidents.
Logs must be stored securely and retained for a minimum period of
[insert retention period], with longer retention periods for critical
systems.
Logs must capture sufficient information to facilitate incident
response and forensic analysis.
Monitoring
Continuous monitoring of logs and network traffic must be
implemented to detect and respond to security threats and anomalies.
Monitoring tools and systems must be regularly updated and
maintained to ensure their effectiveness.
Authorized personnel must be responsible for monitoring and
responding to alerts and incidents.
Access Control of Logs
Access to logs and monitoring systems must be restricted to
authorized personnel only.
Access privileges should be based on the principle of least privilege,
and personnel must undergo training on the responsible use of
monitoring tools.
Safeguarding Logs
Encrypt log data during storage and transmission.
Define and enforce log retention policies to retain logs for necessary
durations.
Aggregate logs in a centralized system for better control.
Conduct routine audits of log files for anomalies.
Use secure protocols for log data transmission.
Auditing and Testing
The administrators of the cloud environments and RRS’s Security Team share
the responsibility of auditing and testing the cloud environment with baselines
and standards set forth in RRS’s policies and applicable compliance objectives.

name: Code of Conduct
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy outlines the behavior, ethics, and security standards all employees
and contractors must follow at RRS.
Obey Relevant Laws and Act Ethically
RRS requires everyone to act within the law and maintain ethical conduct, as all
behavior reflects on the organization.
Laws
All relevant local, national, and international laws and regulations and any other
relevant ones not specifically mentioned by name must be followed.
Ethics
Everyone should act with honesty, integrity, responsible behavior, and
competence. Additionally, everyone should always act in good faith and with
due diligence.
Confidentiality
Employees and contractors with access to confidential information at RRS
must always keep it confidential unless authorized to reveal it for approved
business or legal reasons. Company confidential data should never be used for
personal gain or the benefit of anyone outside of RRS .
Commitment to RRS
During work hours, everyone must always be sober, competent, and able to work
professionally. Outside activities should not interfere with work commitments,
hurt RRS’s reputation, or compete with its business interests.
Avoiding Conflicts of Interest
All employees and contractors must avoid situations that could be a conflict of
interest or appear to be one. They must ensure they have no conflicts of
interest in their personal lives or business relationships and avoid even the
impression that they could have a conflict of interest. They should disclose any
possible conflicts of interest, even if unintentional or believed to be possible to
keep separate from work at RRS.
Maintaining a Safe and Healthy Work Environment
At RRS, we prioritize adherence to industry health and safety laws to maintain a
secure work environment. Additionally, we foster respect and dignity for all,
prohibiting harassment or discrimination. This zero-tolerance stance includes
but is not limited to, bullying, verbal, physical, or psychological abuse.
Misconduct may result in disciplinary action, including termination.
We encourage all organization members to report any perceived misconduct. A
professional response will swiftly follow each report, with an assurance of no
retaliation for good-faith reporting or supporting others in doing so. Together,
we uphold a safe, respectful, and healthy workplace.
Participating in Information Security
Information security is a priority at RRS. Everyone must follow all security
policies, be alert for potential security breaches, participate in all security
education and tests, and keep security in mind when deploying services.
Holding Oneself Accountable
All employees and contractors are accountable for their actions, and everyone
must always follow this code of conduct.

name: Data Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy defines practices to secure and manage data throughout its lifecycle.
It includes data encryption, data retention, data classification, disaster recovery,
data backups, key management, data flow management, and non-repudiation
practices. This policy applies to all data stored on systems and media owned,
leased, or otherwise provided by RRS, regardless of their location. It must be
read and followed by all employees, contractors, the Security Team, IT
personnel, and others charged with managing devices or data storage that
store or process confidential information.
Data Inventory and Mapping
RRS maintains an inventory of all data or data sets, including sensitive and
regulated data. This inventory helps identify, document, and locate data within
the organization. The inventory includes the name of the data set, its
classification, handling requirements, backup requirements, data retention
requirements, and legal requirements. It also includes other relevant
information to ensure a clear understanding of data types and handling
requirements.
Data Owners
Data owners are identified and responsible for defining data classification,
access controls, handling procedures, and ensuring compliance with relevant
regulations and standards. This responsibility includes deciding on and
overseeing the implementation of appropriate security measures to protect
data throughout its lifecycle.
Data Loss Prevention (DLP) Program
A DLP program is in place to prevent unauthorized or accidental transmission or
disclosure of sensitive data outside the network. It includes monitoring and
enforcing policies related to data access, usage, and transfer, along with
techniques such as encryption, data classification, and user activity monitoring.
Data Retention and Destruction
RRS retains data for lawful business purposes and destroys data that is no
longer required in a secure manner. Retention and destruction procedures are
reviewed and updated regularly to ensure compliance with evolving legal,
regulatory, and business requirements.
Key Management
Key management practices are in place to generate, store, distribute, and
revoke encryption keys used to secure sensitive data. This ensures the
confidentiality, integrity, and availability of encryption keys throughout their
lifecycle.
Data Encryption at Rest and in Transit
All confidential, private, and sensitive data stored on devices and media must
be encrypted. Data is also encrypted during transmission to prevent
unauthorized access. Appropriate encryption practices, including AES-256, are
adhered to for both data at rest and in transit.
Attribute Management
Procedures are in place to manage and maintain attributes of systems, such as
access control lists, permissions, and configurations. These procedures ensure
that the systems are correctly configured and secure.
Data Flow Management
RRS documents and manages data flows to ensure security and data privacy.
This includes identifying the data that flows within the organization, the
systems that process and store it, and the individuals or entities with access to
it.
Non-Repudiation
Non-repudiation measures are implemented to ensure the integrity and
authenticity of data or information exchanged between parties. This prevents a
sender or receiver from denying that they sent a message, performed an
action, or received a message.
Backups and Disaster Recovery
Data backup procedures are in place to ensure the availability of data in the
event of a disaster or data loss. In addition, a disaster recovery plan is in place to
ensure the continuity of operations and the swift recovery of data.

name: Endpoint and Configuration Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy provides guidance for all endpoints that access other systems and
data, including workstations, servers, and cloud computing devices, including all
endpoints owned, leased, or otherwise controlled by RRS. See the Bring Your
Own Device (BYOD) Policy for employee-owned devices. Appropriate measures
must be taken when using workstations to ensure the confidentiality, integrity,
and availability of sensitive data.
Requirements
RRS implements physical and technical safeguards to prevent or mitigate
attacks against all endpoints that access other systems and data, including
workstations, servers, cloud computing devices, etc.
The following configurations and practices are required on all company-
controlled endpoints. Exceptions to this list are required to be approved by the
Security Officer and tracked in the asset inventory. A detailed configuration
baseline for each operating system used at RRS must be maintained and
strictly enforced on all company-owned endpoints. Baselines must be
appropriate for the use case of the endpoint and must consider the
configurations and practices below.
Configuration
The following configurations should be made on all devices:
The use of privileged utility programs or any software that requires
administrative privilege to run must be limited and controlled.
Auto-update must be enabled for the operating system that is currently
installed to ensure security patches are applied promptly.
Auto-update must be enabled on all software and applications installed on
all devices to ensure security patches are applied promptly.
Web browsers must be kept up to date.
The device’s storage must be encrypted using an encryption mechanism
approved by the Security Team.
All devices must have their internal clock synced through time-syncing
technologies such as Network Time Protocol (NTP) or the device’s default
time-syncing settings.
An inactivity timer must be set on the device’s display to turn on the
screensaver or lock the screen after at most five minutes of inactivity.
The device must require a password on boot, waking from sleep, or after
the screensaver is enabled.
The built-in firewall or a third-party firewall approved by the Security Team
must be installed and enabled.
The device must be configured so that it does not automatically run
content from connected removable media.
A backup service must be utilized on all devices that store important
information that is not otherwise backed up.
The device must have antivirus software installed and configured according
to the antivirus section later in this policy.
All passwords on all devices must comply with the Access Control Policy.
All sharing settings must be disabled unless they are required, and any
exceptions must be documented.
HTTP and NFS server functionality must be disabled on all workstations
unless required.
Devices must be set up so they cannot be started or woken from sleep
mode over the network unless required.
Devices must be configured to ensure that software can only be installed
with an administrator password.
All vendor-supplied default administrator accounts must be disabled or
changed.
All default passwords must be changed when configuring any new
endpoints before the system is used for its intended purpose.
Systems that do require antivirus software must be periodically checked
manually for malware and vulnerabilities.
All systems storing cardholder data must be encrypted at rest.
File name extensions on devices must be made visible when possible.
Practices
Users must:
Limit access to devices to only authorized users.
Lock the screen or set it to sleep mode when leaving a device for any
period of time.
Ensure food and drink are consumed away from devices or in containers
with a secured lid to prevent spillage.
Only install licensed software approved by the Security Team on devices.
Antivirus
RRS requires antivirus software on all company-owned and controlled
endpoints. The following guidelines must be followed at all times:
Approved antivirus software must be used on all devices at all times.
Real-time protection must be activated at all times.
Antivirus solutions must be configured to automatically scan removable
media when connected.
Antivirus solutions will check for updates at least daily.
Antivirus solutions will update when updates are available.
A full antivirus scan is done on a weekly basis.
Ensure Users’ Compliance
The Security Team is responsible for ensuring compliance with this policy and
must develop and follow a defined process to ensure endpoints are configured
and used in compliance with this policy.

name: Governance
content:
skline on January 14th, 2026 14:21 EST
Introduction and Scope
This policy explains RRS’s information governance program. It gives an overview
of the organization’s mission and objectives for privacy and security and
outlines the key roles and responsibilities related to data privacy and
information security. The policy is designed to provide structure and
consistency to the information security and privacy program at the highest
level of the organization and to establish the team structure as it relates to
information security and data privacy.
This policy applies to all employees and contractors of RRS who have access to
RRS information, including customer information. Every employee and
contractor plays a role in ensuring the confidentiality, availability, and integrity
of this information. Everyone who has access to personal information is
controlled or processed by RRS is responsible for ensuring that data privacy is
respected and handled in accordance with the organization’s policies,
procedures, and applicable laws and regulations.
This policy defines:
The mission statement for information security and privacy
The scope and charter of the information security and privacy program
Roles and responsibilities related to the program
High-level security and privacy guidelines and requirements
Information security and privacy training and awareness
Authority and access control
Separation of duties
Legal and regulatory requirements
Data support and operations
Disciplinary actions
This policy and all other policies and procedures established by RRS, make up
the organization’s information security and privacy program. If you have any
questions, comments, or concerns about the content of this policy or any other
related documents, please contact X (this is a variable that the customer would
add).
Information Security and Privacy Mission Statement
RRS is dedicated to providing its customers with the highest level of
information security and data privacy within its means while complying with
regional, national, and international regulations, standards, and industry best
practices.
Information Security and Privacy Scope and Charter
The following list of items outlines the scope and charter of RRS’s information
security and privacy program:
Maintain the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer
information and all personal information shared within the scope of
operations of RRS, including personal information shared by employees,
contractors, customers, users, and other stakeholders.
Ensure continuous compliance with global, regional, and industry-specific
regulations and standards.
Maintain compliance with information security and data privacy obligations
set out in terms of service agreements and other contracts with third
parties such as contractors, subcontractors, suppliers, and customers.
Ensure that all tools, systems, and other information assets owned or
operated by RRS are securely managed and maintained in compliance with
industry best practices, standards, and regulations.
Ensure that all employees, contractors, service providers, and suppliers
follow the policies, procedures, and controls developed within this
information security and privacy program.
RRS leverages an Information Security Management Program (ISMP) based on
the following standard(s):
Service Organization Controls 2 (SOC 2)
Center for Internet Security Critical Security Controls V7.1 (CIS CSC)
Implementation Group 1
Center for Internet Security Critical Security Controls V7.1 (CIS CSC)
Implementation Group 2
ISO 27001
NIST SP 800-171
NIST SP 800-53
NIST CSF
Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI-DSS)
RRS must comply with all applicable privacy and data protection regulations
that apply to RRS, based on local, regional, and international laws as applicable.
RRS must meet its responsibilities for protecting data with respect to the
rights of individuals; therefore, RRS will follow these directives:
Comply with and be aware of all applicable privacy or data protection rules
and regulations.
Comply with all laws and associated regulations or rules in all jurisdictions
where business is conducted.
The following rules and regulations apply to RRS:
General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)
Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA)
Sarbanes-Oxley Act (SOX)
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
RRS has a centralized, top-down approach to managing information security
and data privacy, which includes clear roles and responsibilities for its key
stakeholders.
Board of Directors
The board of directors is responsible for ensuring that RRS has a formal written
security program that is appropriate for the organization based on risk
tolerance goals, legal requirements, and customer expectations. The board
ensures that the organizational structure and leadership of the company
enforce the security program and that outside validation and testing are
leveraged to validate the program’s success.
The board of directors meets regularly to ensure appropriate organizational
governance. The board prioritizes the privacy and security of customer,
personal, financial, and other important information and communicates these
priorities throughout the organization. The board is aware of the high-level
security and privacy obligations. It holds leadership accountable for ensuring
appropriate measures and controls are designed, deployed, maintained, and
audited regularly to ensure RRS complies with regulatory requirements, industry
standards, and customer expectations.
CEO
As the primary executive leader of RRS, the CEO is responsible for ensuring
that the organizational privacy and security initiatives are appropriately staffed
and budgeted. The CEO must also ensure that internal auditing and reporting
are received and reviewed regularly and that the board of directors receives an
executive report highlighting the company’s performance and compliance with
its requirements and obligations.
The CEO is responsible for ensuring that an appropriately skilled and
empowered individual is assigned as the Security Officer for the organization.
The CEO must ensure that this person has the authority and resources to
develop, deploy, and manage the security program as per the risk tolerances set
by senior management, customer requirements, and applicable laws and
regulations.
Security Officer
The Security Officer is responsible for developing, deploying, and maintaining
the information security program and the creation and managing the Security
Team. The Security Officer will:
Assist department heads in creating and managing departmental security
policies.
Coordinate audits to make sure security policies are being enforced.
Coordinate RRS’s Incident Response Team.
Represent the department on the RRS Security Team.
Managing and scheduling risk assessments
Communicating the risk management program throughout the
organization
Leading mitigation efforts
Oversee data privacy compliance.
Communicate new or revised policies, procedures, and standards on behalf of
the
Security Team to all employees.
Review security policies, procedures, and standards no less than annually
and revise them as needed to reflect changes in regulatory, legal, and
contractual requirements.
Security Team
The Security Team, led by the Security Officer, is collectively responsible for
ensuring that risk assessments are comprehensive, accurate, transparent, and
void of conflict. The team is responsible for ensuring that key perspectives and
inputs are at the table when assessing impact, threat, and risk and that
remediation efforts and resource requests are clearly and comprehensively
presented to senior management and key stakeholders.
The Security Team develops, updates, and approves security policies and
procedures. During this process, the team considers the business needs and
security concerns of RRS. The team reviews any deviation from
standards/policies and then approves or denies the exceptions.
Privacy Officer
RRS assigns the Privacy Officer role based on professional qualities. The Privacy
Officer must have expert knowledge of data protection law and practices and
have no conflicts of interest. The Privacy Officer will:
Act as the Data Protection Officer (DPO) if GDPR requires one or if the
organization determines that it is in its best interest to have one. GDPR
requires public organizations to appoint a DPO if core activities involve
processing personal data on a large scale or if they consistently process
highly sensitive data.
Work with regulators to help ensure compliance.
Ensure the training program covers appropriate privacy regulations and
requirements.
Keep up with changes in law and technology.
Develop, implement, and manage the privacy program.
Incident Response Team
The Incident Response Team creates and periodically tests incident response
plans that document processes to resolve high-risk and high-impact incidents.
All Employees and Contractors
All authorized users, staff members, employees, volunteers, and contractors
who have access to RRS resources are required to:
Read, understand, and follow applicable policies and procedures.
Help protect RRS data and resources from being shared or changed
without permission.
Protect and respect the privacy of personal information.
Operate in compliance with all applicable laws and regulations.
Sign an employment or contractor agreement that clarifies privacy and
security obligations in compliance with RRS’s information security and
privacy program.
Exceptions may be made for contractors with their own information security
and privacy program deemed appropriate by RRS. Details regarding contractors
can be found in the Third-Party Management Policy.
High-Level Security and Privacy Guidelines and
Requirements
All members of RRS must have access to security and privacy policies and
procedures relevant to their roles and responsibilities.
These policies, procedures, and standards must comply with and support
applicable laws, regulations, and contracts.
These policies, procedures, and standards will be reviewed no less than
annually and revised as needed to reflect changes in regulatory, legal, and
contractual requirements.
The Security Team must approve any exceptions to the minimum security
requirements, and compensating controls must be in place.
Requests for permission to deviate from the policy must be made with the
Security Officer. All approvals will be documented along with rational risk
considerations and compensating controls.
All employees and those that act on behalf of RRS must display integrity
and make ethical decisions in the execution of their relevant roles and
responsibilities.
Information Security and Privacy Training and
Awareness
All employees and contractors must be informed about information security
best practices and privacy obligations. RRS must ensure that:
Training on best practices in information security and privacy policies,
procedures, and relevant threats to RRS is provided. Employee and
contractor information security and privacy awareness training will include
the secure use of information security assets. It will cover topics such as
secure authentication, identifying social engineering attacks, sensitive data
handling, unintentional data exposure, relevant privacy regulations,
identifying and reporting incidents, and breach notification requirements.
Information security and privacy awareness training is mandatory for
employee/contractor onboarding and must be provided at least annually.
Performance reviews will include participation and performance in the
information security and privacy awareness training.
Addressing Skills Gap
To ensure that the security, privacy, and Incident Response Team members have
the necessary skills to develop, implement, manage, and measure the progress
and performance of the information security and privacy program, a skills gap
analysis should be conducted and updated annually. Information security
policies and procedures, along with awareness training, should ensure that all
employees are aware of their information security and privacy obligations and
responsibilities. If individuals assigned to security and privacy functions lack the
required skills or knowledge, advanced training or third-party participation is
required.
Enforcement
There is an established and enforced system to address behavior, actions, or
inaction that results in a security breach, breach of contractual obligations, or
failure to comply with policies and procedures. A clear process for disciplinary
and corrective measures for a breach of contractual obligations is provided to all
employees.
At onboarding, annually, and when their roles change, all employees must
read, understand, and sign off on the relevant policies.
Employees who do not follow all policies may face disciplinary action, such
as denial of access, legal penalties, or dismissal.
In case an employee causes a data breach, the severity of disciplinary action
will be decided on a case-by-case basis after a thorough investigation into
the manner and intent of the breach.
Any employee who becomes aware of a violation of company policies must
report it to a supervisor or other authorized representative.
If employees or third parties wish to be exempt from any part of this
policy, they must first obtain permission from RRS.
Further details on disciplinary actions are available in the Human Resource
Security Policy as well as in each employee agreement.

name: Human Resource Security
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy outlines RRS’s human resources requirements concerning
information security and data privacy at each stage of employment or
engagement. This applies during onboarding, throughout employment or
independent contractor engagements, and upon role change or termination.
All personnel responsible for HR at RRS must adhere to this policy. It governs
the requirements for all employees and independent contractors hired by RRS.
Contracted companies must adhere to the third-party vendor requirements
per the Third-Party Management Policy. Agreements must clearly state the
vendor’s security and privacy requirements. Independent contractors or small
companies without their own security and privacy policies must commit to
adhering to our policies, as would an employee with equivalent access and
responsibilities.
Onboarding
Candidates for employment must meet our high standards. They must sign an
agreement and pass all screening requirements before accessing confidential
information or critical systems.
Screening
Candidates undergo a standard background check, including character
reference and criminal background checks. They also undergo an employment
competency check, which includes CV/Resume vetting and job knowledge
testing.
Employment Contract
Candidates sign an agreement before starting work. The contract includes
identifying relevant parties, engagement dates, type of
employment/engagement, role and responsibilities, employee benefits,
termination conditions and process, intellectual property rights, security and
data privacy obligations, and company security and privacy policies and
procedures.
During Employment
All personnel must be clear about their roles and responsibilities. Performance
is monitored and managed, with corrective measures applied when necessary.
Managers must reinforce these responsibilities annually and when role changes
occur.
Security and Privacy Responsibility Awareness and
Training
All personnel must receive role-appropriate training and participate in an
awareness program. Training must align with their roles’ security and privacy
requirements. Records of training, including completion dates, test results, and
any certification achieved, will be maintained and regularly reviewed.
Disciplinary Actions
A system is in place to correct behavior resulting in a security breach, breach of
contract, or policy deviation. Corrective measures include policy sign-off during
onboarding and role changes, possible disciplinary actions for policy violations,
mandatory reporting of policy violations to the Security Team, and the Security
Team’s approval for policy exemption requests.
Role Change and Termination
Obligations relating to information security and data privacy continue post-
termination or role change. Specific checklists will be developed and utilized
during these transitions. Upon termination, all accounts are suspended pending
any necessary review or recovery of critical data. All assets must be returned,
including equipment, intellectual property, and access keys. Returned
equipment will be reviewed, data recovered if necessary and reset to a baseline
state for reissuance.
Employee Handbook
Upon commencement of their role, every employee and contractor will receive
a copy of the Employee Handbook, providing detailed guidance on terms,
conditions, code of conduct, and disciplinary procedures. Acknowledgment of
receipt and understanding of the handbook’s contents is required. Regular
updates will be communicated to all staff.

name: Identity and Access Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
Identity management and access management are two concepts that come
together to encompass Identity and Access Management (IAM). Identity
management relates to information, traits, or items that are uniquely
attributable to an individual; the individual is, has, or knows something that
proves their identity. Access management or access control relates to
determining which individuals have access to what systems, accounts, data, etc.
This policy defines the requirements of RRS, governing acceptable methods of
determining a user’s identity and how access control is managed for these
identities on networks and services.
Authentication Services
Single Sign-On
Third-party authentication services are approved for use at RRS from the
following provider(s):
Google
No other authentication service providers are approved at this time. All
employees or independent contractors at RRS must use this service for all
systems that support it.
Multi-Factor Authentication
In authentication terms, a factor can be considered something someone is, has,
or knows. Multi-factor authentication (MFA) means that a user has to provide
proof of two or more factors. A username and password plus a token, device, or
fingerprint could be considered MFA.
MFA must be enforced to access accounts with higher privileges, such as
administrative accounts at RRS, as well as any other accounts that store or
process confidential information.
Authorization Services
Access Control Lists
Sensitive data, documents, files, and environments will have strong access
controls. These access controls should be managed and enforced based on a
“need-to-know” basis, meaning that only those who “need-to-know” or have
access to a sensitive asset will have access to it. This policy will be enforced
with the help of Access Control Lists (ACLs).
ACLs will govern access to shared folders, files, and documents. ACLs should
also be used to govern who has access to shared cloud storage assets, such as
Google Drive, Dropbox, or OneDrive.
Role-Based Access Control
Systems that house, process, and have access to confidential data will enforce
role-based access control (RBAC) models. These RBAC systems will also follow
the requirements detailed below:
Assign the level of access to data users require based on their role.
Have a system-generated list of all users with access to electronic
protected health information (ePHI) and what data they access across all
systems that handle ePHI.
Have access reports of all occurrences of accessing ePHI over the last 12
months.
Have a system-generated list of all new hires over the past 12 months.
Use universally unique identifiers (UUIDs) for every account and individual
that has access to ePHI.
Privileged Access Control
Privileged access is given to administrators of systems, applications, network
devices, DevOps, or cloud environments for enhanced functionality or
administration. Users who need to access administrative features will be
provisioned a separate administrative account for each instance of
administrative privilege.
Administrator accounts for all systems and services assets belonging to
RRS will be created on a case-by-case basis, must be authorized by the
asset owner, and are for individual roles that require administrative
privileges for the target system in order to conduct the role.
Administrative accounts for all systems and services will not be used for
day-to-day usage for any system or service.
Multi-factor authentication must be enforced on all administrative
accounts, and the user must input multi-factor authentication to access
any administrative accounts.
A documented procedure must be in place to outline the process for
authorizing access to confidential information.
At least two individuals must have administrative access and knowledge to
manage every critical system, application, network device(s), DevOps, or cloud
environment. This ensures at least one fallback administrator should the
primary administrator become unavailable.
User Management Services
Account Provisioning
New users will be provisioned accounts needed to perform their duties
associated with their role with RRS. Provisioning will be conducted at
onboarding and is the responsibility of the Security Team.
Each account that is provisioned for users in all environments must be
documented and accounted for by ensuring there is a need for that user to
have that account and what privileges that user needs.
Account Deprovisioning
All users who depart or change roles at RRS will have their accounts reviewed.
Accounts no longer required will be deactivated.
Users leaving RRS must have all accounts deactivated before or during
departure/exit interviews.
Account deprovisioning is the responsibility of the Security Team.
Disabling Dormant or Unassociated Accounts
Employees sometimes take extended periods off, such as parental leave. That
being considered, accounts that are dormant for these reasons should be
disabled after a defined period of inactivity. Accounts that do not have a valid
reason to be inactive will be deactivated after 90 days of inactivity.
Accounts that cannot be attributed to a specific user for a specific business use
case will be deactivated.
Passwords
All employees are responsible for creating and administering their passwords
following this policy’s guidelines. For all services where a password change is
not possible through self-service, all password requests must be sent to the
Security Team.
Unique Passwords or Passphrases
Unique passwords or passphrases must be used for all accounts and services at
RRS. Passwords for personal accounts are strictly prohibited for use within RRS.
Best Practice
Employees and contractors at RRS must memorize two main passphrases: - one
to access their laptop or desktop and one to access the company-approved
password manager. For all other systems, single sign-on (SSO) or the password
manager is leveraged to access those services.
Passphrase/Password Construction Guidelines
Users should use a password manager to build strong passwords. If, for any
reason, users cannot use the password manager, they should use the following
advice to create a strong password or passphrase:
The password or passphrase should be memorable to the user but not
easily guessable by anyone who has information about the user, such as the
user’s date of birth, dog’s name, or the date on which the user created the
password.
The password or passphrase should be complex. To create a passphrase,
the user should think of two or three long and unrelated words. The user
should be able to remember them and can replace some letters with
numbers and symbols. For instance, if the two words are ballistic and
communication, the passphrase could be `B4lli$ticC0mmunic4ti0n`. The
way to remember this would be to think of the words Ballistic and
Communication with the first letters capitalized. The a’s are 4’s, the s is a $,
and the o’s are 0’s.
Users should avoid clichés and quotations. For example,
`ToBeOrNotToBeThatIsTheQuestion3905` is a weak passphrase because it
can be easily identified from the Shakespeare play Hamlet, even with the
numbers on the end.
Password Complexity Requirements
Use a password manager to build strong passwords. If users cannot use
password management software, they must still use the following rules to
create a strong password or passphrase:
Users should use a minimum of 12 characters.
Users should not use sequential and repetitive characters (e.g., `1234567` or
`aaaaaaaaa`).
Users should avoid context-specific passwords, like using parts of a login
username or the name of the website or service. For example, if the
vendor’s website is acme.com, users should never create a password like
`AcmeSecret1981`.
Users should not use commonly used passwords (e.g., `p@ssw0rd` or
`qwerty`).
Users should not include information that someone could easily guess
based on their identity. Phone numbers, dates of birth, anniversaries,
children or pet names, and home addresses should not be used in
passwords.
Users should use symbols and punctuation in their passwords. They should
include at least some of the following: lowercase letters, uppercase letters,
numbers, punctuation, symbols, emojis, and non-English characters.
Passwords must be updated every 12 months.
Default Passwords
When provisioning a new device, asset, or account, all default passwords will be
changed from the default password to one that meets the RRS “Password
Complexity Requirements” in this policy.
Access Reviews
The Security Team will conduct an access control review regularly to ensure
compliance with this policy, and that access is on a need-to-know basis.

name: Incident Reporting
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy aims to guide all employees and contractors of RRS on how to
identify, report, and assist in the prompt resolution of any security or privacy
incidents. The policy applies to those who have access to the organization’s
data and systems.
This policy must be followed in case of an actual or potential security or privacy
incident. A security or privacy incident is defined as any event that could
potentially impact the confidentiality, integrity, or availability of information,
systems, hardware, or software. It can also refer to any other event that may
negatively affect the company, its people, customers, stakeholders, or systems.
Being Aware of Possible Security Incidents
Everyone plays a crucial role in identifying security incidents. Therefore, it is
important for all employees and contractors to be vigilant in detecting possible
security or privacy incidents. Some examples of suspicious activity include:
Unauthorized access to a secure area or network
Theft or damage to company hardware or assets
Signs of fraud, such as inaccurate information in databases or other records
Unexpected email or text messages from a superior or coworker that go
against the organization’s security policies or request the transfer of
money or gift cards
Phishing attempts, such as emails asking employees to click on a link and
submit their username and password
Unusual system behavior such as unscheduled reboots, unusual errors in
system log files, or security event log notifications such as file integrity
alerts or intrusion detection alarms.
Reporting Security Incidents Promptly
A quick response to possible security breaches is crucial to the organization.
Therefore, all employees and contractors must immediately report any security
or privacy incident or potential incident. In case of an incident, the following
steps should be taken:
1. Report the incident to the designated contact (to be provided).
2. Do not discuss the incident with anyone except your supervisor or the
Security Team unless directed otherwise by your supervisor or the Security
Team.
3. While waiting for the Security Team’s response, document all known
information about the incident, such as the date, time, nature, and
suspicious event or behavior that led to the report.
Other Considerations
In case of an imminent threat to human life or property, employees should
call 911. Otherwise, they should follow the above contact and notification
process.
If an employee is unsure whether a situation is a security incident, they
should still contact the Security Team immediately. The team will evaluate
the situation. It’s better to be cautious than to miss a security breach.
The individual who discovers and reports a potential incident must keep it
confidential until directed otherwise by the Security Team.
For customer-facing systems, a process for customers and users to report
potential security or performance issues must be in place, clearly
articulated, and available to end users.
In case a computer or other endpoint is involved, employees must ensure that
nothing changes on the computer’s system by:
1. Leaving the computer on.
2. Leaving all programs open and running.
3. Disconnect the computer from the network:
Wireless network: Disable the computer’s wireless access.
Wired network: Pull out the network cable from the back of the
computer.
Helping the Security Team in the Incident Response
After receiving an incident report, the Security Team will evaluate the incident’s
severity and develop an appropriate plan. Witnesses should assist the Security
Team in resolving the issue as requested.
Participating in the Debriefing Process
If requested, the person who reported the incident must participate in the
debriefing process, which will take place within one week of the security
incident.
Participating in Incident Response Training and Tests
This policy requires that all employees at RRS participate in the organization’s
incident response training. This training ensures that everyone is prepared for
both tests and real-life incidents.
The Security Team will hold a mock incident at least once a year. During this
test, all employees must treat it as seriously as an actual incident and follow the
incident response plan carefully. Employees must attend and participate in any
follow-up sessions. The Security Team will decide on the type of security
incident to test, such as a physical security breach, network breach, or code
error.

name: Incident Response for Security Team
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy provides guidelines for swift resolution of security and privacy
incidents within RRS.
This policy, applicable to the Security Team (referred to as the Incident
Response Team), outlines the management and response to security incidents,
particularly those impacting critical systems or data. Any deviations from the
policy require approval from the Security Officer and senior management.
Roles and Responsibilities
The Security Team is tasked with incident response. Clear delegation of roles
and responsibilities is vital, including decision-making, public relations,
communication with legal authorities, crisis management, and incident
management. Updated contact information should be maintained for:
Security Team members
Public Relations or designated communication officers
Relevant law enforcement, government agencies, and threat intelligence-
sharing partners
Any relevant parties
Senior management and CEO
Incident Response Plans
Comprehensive response plans are required for all high-to-severe business
risks identified via the risk management program, adhering to this policy. In the
absence of a specific plan, this policy will guide the response.
Incident Reporting
A Security Team member must be accessible 24/7 to initiate incident
responses. Reliable primary and alternate contact methods should be
documented and accessible to all employees. The Security Team should ensure
multiple notification systems for critical assets or systems, including:
Automated security notifications
Employee and customer reporting processes
All employees must be capable of identifying and reporting incidents
effectively. The person discovering the incident must maintain confidentiality
until advised by the Security Team.
Incident Assessment
The initial assessment determines if an incident warrants a formal response. If
not, the incident is logged and assigned for resolution. If it does, an Incident
Response Team is formed.
Incident Response Team
The team may comprise of:
Security Team
CTO
IT staff
Legal, HR, and other relevant staff
Outside security experts
Cybersecurity insurance provider
NOTE: Legal involvement is critical for incidents involving potential data
breaches. Mark all correspondence “ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGE”.
Incident Response Steps
1. Safety
Immediate response prioritizes human life and safety.
2. Availability and Customer Impact
Uptime requirements must be met for customer-facing systems.
3. Scope
The team quickly establishes the incident’s scope, identifying affected systems
and data.
4. Containment and Documentation
After ensuring safety and availability, containment actions are initiated within
24 hours. Actions include:
Isolating affected systems
Restricting compromised system access
Terminating affected accounts
Changing credentials
Blocking IP addresses
NOTE: Don’t power down compromised systems to preserve forensic evidence.
5. Evidence Preservation
The team devises a plan to preserve evidence, considering the need for
availability restoration.
6. Investigation
The team investigates the incident, identifies causes, and determines
preventive actions.
7. Notification
Legal and senior management assist in identifying parties to be notified, which
may include customers, vendors, contractors, employees, ISPs, authorities,
affected individuals, and media.
8. Root Cause Analysis
A detailed root cause analysis is performed, and results are thoroughly
documented.
9. Corrective Actions & Proposals
The team suggests remedies for the incident. Management decides which
recommendations to implement.
10. Corrective Action Implementation
Approved actions are implemented, and results are documented.
11. Post-Implementation Follow-Up
The team performs appropriate post-correction actions.
Social Engineering and Phishing Attempts
Report such attempts to _______, attaching a screenshot of the suspected
attack.
Audit Logging
All RRS systems and networking devices should enable local audit logging. This
supports the Security Team in incident response, investigation, and mitigation,
by facilitating an accurate timeline of events.
Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery
The Security Team is tasked with formulating, maintaining, and testing detailed
incident response and business continuity plans for critical and high-risk items
identified in the risk management process.
Incident Response Testing
An annual tabletop exercise ensures the readiness of the Security Team to
respond to real incidents. Insights gathered are used to refine and improve
response plans.
Confidentiality
It’s crucial that potential security incidents are kept confidential until the
Security Team provides guidance. This includes communications with internal
and external parties, such as employees, contractors, senior management,
customers, law enforcement, and the public.
Special Cases: Social Engineering and Phishing
Attempts
Report any social engineering or phishing attempts to the designated officer
via email, attaching a screenshot of the suspected attempt. The Incident
Response Team will then:
Obtain a copy of the phishing email/text message for analysis.
Test any links and attachments for malicious intent in a quarantined
environment.
Add the sender’s email to company-wide spam filters and blacklist
suspicious domains.
If appropriate, issue a company-wide alert with screenshots of the
phishing email and a reminder of phishing signs.
If login credentials may have been compromised, enforce a password
change in accordance with the Access Control Policy.
Remember, maintaining the integrity and security of RRS is a shared
responsibility. This policy provides a framework for coordinated and effective
incident management and resolution. Please adhere to it diligently.

name: Mobile Device Management
content:
Mobile Device Acceptable Use Policy
Purpose
This policy defines standards, procedures, and restrictions for any and all end users with
legitimate business uses connecting mobile devices to RRS’s corporate network, digital
resources, and data. The mobile device policy applies, but is not limited to, all devices and
accompanying media that fit the following classifications:
Smartphones
Other mobile/cellular phones
Tablets
E-readers
Portable media devices
Portable gaming devices
Laptop/notebook/ultrabook computers
Wearable computing devices
Any other mobile device capable of storing corporate data and connecting to a network
In order to enforce security and remote device management, only devices that meet the
following criteria are allowed to access corporate resources:
Smartphones, tablets, and other devices running Android version 2.3 (Gingerbread) and
higher.
Smartphones and tablets running iOS 5.0 and higher.
Laptops running Windows 7 and higher
Laptops running Mac OS X Cheetah (10.0) and higher
The policy applies to any mobile device that is used to access corporate resources, whether
the device is owned by the user or by the organization.
The primary goal of this policy is to protect the integrity of the confidential client and
business data that resides within RRS’s technology infrastructure, including internal and
external cloud services. This policy intends to prevent this data from being deliberately or
inadvertently stored insecurely on a mobile device or carried over an insecure network where
it could potentially be accessed by unauthorized resources. A breach of this type may result
in loss of information, damage to critical applications, loss of revenue, damage the
company’s public image, breach our data privacy requirements, and violate data privacy laws.
Therefore, all employees, contractors, or personnel using a mobile device connected to
RRS’s corporate network, and/or capable of backing up, storing, or otherwise accessing
corporate data of any type, must adhere to company-defined processes and policies in doing
so.
Applicability
This policy applies to all RRS employees, including full and part-time staff, contractors,
freelancers, and other agents who use any mobile device to access, store, backup, or relocate
any organization or client-specific data. Such access to this confidential data is a privilege,
not a right, and forms the basis of the trust RRS has built with its clients, supply chain
partners, and other constituents. Consequently, employment at RRS does not automatically
guarantee the initial or ongoing ability to use these devices to gain access to corporate
networks and information.
The policy addresses a range of threats to enterprise data, or related to its use, such as:
Threat Description
Device Devices used to transfer or transport work files could be lost or stolen.
Loss
Data Sensitive data is deliberately stolen and sold by an employee or unauthorized third party.
Theft
Malwar Viruses, Trojans, worms, spyware, malware, and other threats could be introduced to or via a
e mobile device.
Compli Loss or theft of financial and/or personal and confidential data could expose the enterprise to
ance the risk of non-compliance with various identity theft and privacy laws.
Addition of new hardware, software, and/or related components to provide additional mobile
device connectivity will be managed at the sole discretion of our IT group. Unauthorized use
of mobile devices to back up, store, and otherwise access any company-related data is strictly
forbidden.
This policy is complementary to any previously implemented policies dealing specifically
with data access, data storage, data movement, and connectivity of devices to any element of
the company network and resources.
Responsibilities
The Security Officer of RRS has the overall responsibility for the confidentiality, integrity,
and availability of corporate data.
All RRS employees are responsible to act in accordance with company policies and
procedures.
Affected Technology
Connectivity of all mobile devices will be centrally managed by RRS’s IT department and
will use authentication and strong encryption measures. Although IT will not directly
manage personal devices purchased by employees, end users are expected to adhere to the
same security protocols when connected to non-corporate equipment. Failure to do so will
result in immediate suspension of all network access privileges so as to protect the
company’s infrastructure.
Policy & Appropriate Use
It is the responsibility of any RRS employee using a mobile device to access corporate
resources to ensure that all security protocols normally used in the management of data on
conventional storage infrastructure are also applied here. It is imperative that any mobile
device that is used to conduct RRS business be used appropriately, responsibly, and ethically.
Failure to do so will result in immediate suspension of that user’s account. Based on this
requirement, the following rules must be observed:
Access Control
1. IT reserves the right to refuse, by physical and non-physical means, the ability to
connect mobile devices to corporate and corporate-connected infrastructure. IT will
engage in such action if such equipment is being used in a way that puts the company’s
systems, data, users, and clients at risk.
2. Prior to initial use on the corporate network or related infrastructure, all mobile devices
must be approved by IT. RRS will maintain a list of approved mobile devices and
related software applications and utilities
3. End users who wish to connect such devices to non-corporate network infrastructure to
gain access to enterprise data must employ, for their devices and related infrastructure,
security measures deemed necessary by the IT department. Enterprise data is not to be
accessed on any hardware that fails to meet RRS’s established enterprise IT security
standards.
4. All personal mobile devices attempting to connect to the corporate network through the
Internet will be inspected by RRS’s IT department. Devices that are not approved by IT,
are not in compliance with IT’s security policies, or represent any threat to the
corporate network or data will not be allowed to connect.
Mobile Device Management (MDM)
1. RRS’s IT department uses Google mobile device management solution to secure mobile
devices and enforce policies remotely. Before connecting a mobile device to corporate
resources, the device must be set to be manageable by Google
2. The mobile device management solution enables IT to take the following actions on
mobile devices: [remote wipe, location tracking, remote lock].
3. Any attempt to contravene or bypass the mobile device management implementation
will result in immediate disconnection from all corporate resources, and there may be
additional consequences in accordance with RRS’s overarching security policy.
Security
1. Employees using mobile devices and related software for network and data access will,
without exception, use secure data management procedures. All mobile devices must be
protected by a strong password; a PIN is not sufficient. All data stored on the device
must be encrypted using strong encryption. See RRS’s password and encryption policy
for additional background. Employees agree never to disclose their passwords to anyone.
2. All users of mobile devices must employ reasonable physical security measures. End
users are expected to secure all such devices against being lost or stolen, whether or not
they are actually in use and/or being carried.
3. Any non-corporate computers used to synchronize or backup data on mobile devices
will have installed up-to-date anti-virus and anti-malware software deemed necessary by
RRS’s IT department.
4. Passwords and other confidential data, as defined by RRS’s IT department, are not to be
stored unencrypted on mobile devices.
5. Any mobile device that is being used to store or access RRS’s data must adhere to the
authentication requirements of RRS’s IT department. In addition, all hardware security
configurations must be pre-approved by RRS’s IT department before any enterprise
data-carrying device can be connected to the corporate network.
6. IT will manage security policies, network, application, and data access centrally using
whatever technology solutions it deems suitable. Any attempt to contravene or bypass
that security implementation will be deemed an intrusion attempt and will be dealt with
in accordance with RRS’s overarching security policy.
7. Employees, contractors, and temporary staff accessing RRS internet resources from a
smartphone or tablet will NOT save their user credentials or internet sessions when
logging in or accessing company resources of any kind.
8. Employees, contractors, and temporary staff will follow all enterprise-sanctioned data
removal procedures to permanently erase company-specific data from such devices once
its use is no longer required.
9. In the event of a lost or stolen mobile device, the user is required to report the incident
to IT immediately. The device will be remotely wiped of all data and locked to prevent
access by anyone other than IT. If the device is recovered, it can be submitted to IT for
re-provisioning. The remote wipe will destroy all data on the device, whether it is
related to company business or personal. The RRS Remote Wipe Waiver, which ensures
that the user understands that personal data may be erased in the rare event of a security
breach, must be agreed to before connecting the device to corporate resources.
10. Usage of location-based services and mobile check-in services, which use GPS
capabilities to share real-time user location with external parties, is prohibited within
the workplace.
11. Usage of a mobile device to capture images, video, or audio, whether native to the
device or through third-party applications, is prohibited within the workplace.
12. Applications that are not approved by IT are not to be used within the workplace or in
conjunction with corporate data.
Hardware& Support
1. IT reserves the right, through policy enforcement and any other means it deems
necessary, to limit the ability of end users to transfer data to and from specific resources
on the enterprise network.
2. Users will make no modifications to the hardware or software that change the nature of
the device in a significant way (e.g. replacing or overriding the operating system, jail-
breaking, rooting) without the express approval of RRS’s IT department.
3. IT will support the connection of mobile devices to corporate resources. On personally
owned devices, IT will not support hardware issues or non-corporate applications.
Organizational Protocol
1. IT can and will establish audit trails, which will be accessed, published, and used
without notice. Such trails will be able to track the attachment of an external device to
the corporate network, and the resulting reports may be used for investigation of
possible breaches and/or misuse. The end user agrees to and accepts that his or her
access and/or connection to RRS’s networks may be monitored to record dates, times,
duration of access, etc. in order to identify unusual usage patterns or other suspicious
activity. The status of the device, including location, IP address, Serial Number, IMEI,
may also be monitored. This monitoring is necessary in order to identify
accounts/computers that may have been compromised by external parties or users who
are not complying with RRS’s policies.
2. The end user agrees to immediately report to his/her manager and RRS’s IT department
any incident or suspected incidents of unauthorized data access, data loss, and/or
disclosure of company resources, databases, networks, etc.
3. RRS will not reimburse employees if they choose to purchase their own mobile devices.
Users will not be allowed to expense mobile network usage costs.
4. Every mobile device user will be entitled and expected to attend a training session about
this policy. While a mobile device user will not be granted access to corporate resources
using a mobile device without accepting the terms and conditions of this policy,
employees are entitled to decline signing this policy if they do not understand the policy
or are uncomfortable with its contents.
5. Any questions relating to this policy should be directed to Sean in IT, at
skline@accessrrs.com.

name: Physical Media
content:
RRS
Disposal of Media Policy and Procedures
1.0 Purpose
The purpose of this policy is to outline the proper disposal of media (physical or
electronic) at RRS
These rules are in place to protect sensitive and classified information,
employees and RRS. Inappropriate disposal of RRS and media may put
employees, RRS at risk.
2.0 Scope
This policy applies to all RRS employees, contractors, temporary staff, and other
workers at
RRS, with access to FBI CJIS systems and/or data, sensitive and classified data,
and media.
This policy applies to all equipment that processes, stores, and/or transmits PII,
PHI and classified and sensitive data that is owned or leased by RRS.
3.0 Policy
When no longer usable, hard drives, diskettes, tape cartridges, CDs, ribbons,
hard copies, print-outs, and
other similar items used to process, store and/or transmit PII, PHI and classified
and sensitive data shall
be properly disposed of in accordance with measures established by RRS.
Physical media (print-outs and other physical media) shall be disposed of by
one of the following methods:
1) shredding using RRS issued shredders.
2) incineration using RRS incinerators or witnessed by RRS personnel onsite
at agency or at contractor incineration site, if conducted by non-authorized
personnel.
Electronic media (hard-drives, tape cartridge, CDs, printer ribbons, flash drives,
printer and copier
Hard-drives, etc.) shall be disposed of by one of these methods:
1) Overwriting (at least 3 times) - an effective method of clearing data from
magnetic media. As
the name implies, overwriting uses a program to write (1s, 0s, or a combination
of both) onto
the location of the media where the file to be sanitized is located.
2) Degaussing - a method to magnetically erase data from magnetic media. Two
types of
degaussing exist: strong magnets and electric degausses. Note that common
magnets (e.g.,
those used to hang a picture on a wall) are fairly weak and cannot effectively
degauss magnetic
media.
3) Destruction – a method of destroying magnetic media. As the name implies,
destruction of
magnetic media is to physically dismantle by methods of crushing,
disassembling, etc.,
ensuring that the platters have been physically destroyed so that no data can
be pulled.
IT systems that have been used to process, store, or transmit PII, PHI and/or
sensitive and classified
information shall not be released from RRS‘ control until the equipment has
been sanitized and
all stored information has been cleared using one of the above methods.
4.0 Enforcement
Any employee found to have violated this policy may be subject to disciplinary
action, up to and including
termination.

name: Physical Security
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy should be read and followed by all employees and independent
contractors responsible for office facilities, IT equipment, print media, and all
other data under the organization’s control. This policy defines the processes
and guidelines for securing the utilities and managing physical access to
safeguard personnel, prevent unauthorized access, ensure data integrity, and
maintain the availability of systems and data.
Office Physical Security
The office space is secured to ensure only authorized individuals can access the
facility:
Doors, windows, and other entry points are secured with locks, security
doors, and biometric access controls and are continuously monitored by
security cameras, access logs, and security personnel.
All entries are locked or monitored during office hours to ensure only
authorized individuals enter the facility.
Visitors are logged in and monitored during visits.
Physical access logs are maintained to track the movement of people and
assets within the facility.
Protected Areas
Servers, network equipment, filing cabinets, and other storage areas of
confidential information or information assets must be secured.
Access to these areas is restricted using physical barriers and monitored
via security cameras, access logs, and security personnel.
Delivery and removal of assets are tracked as they enter and leave these
areas to ensure their security.
Equipment and utilities are protected from physical and logical threats that
could compromise their availability, reliability, and integrity.
Clean Desk Requirements
All personnel are required to ensure the following:
All protected data, whether in electronic or paper form, must always be
secured in their work area.
All devices must be locked when left alone, or the workspace is unoccupied.
Any protected information must be removed from the desk and locked in a
drawer or cabinet when the work area is unoccupied and at the end of the
day.
Keys used to access protected information must be secured at all times.
They must not be left unattended at any time.
Passwords must never be written down.
Documents containing protected data must be disposed of based on the
Data Retention and Destruction Policy.
Physical media, such as storage devices, are securely protected during use
and disposed of based on the Data Retention and Destruction Policy.
Network Security
The security and confidentiality of the information technology infrastructure
within the office environment are ensured:
All network devices are kept up to date with security-related firmware
updates.
All wireless network access points use an approved Wi-Fi encryption
algorithm and strong passwords.
All wireless devices are audited for proper configuration, and only devices
using WPA2-AES or later versions are permitted.
A separate network or segment is established for guest network access.
This network has its own security baseline, including authentication and
access control requirements.
System maintenance is conducted regularly. Employees are routinely
trained on their roles and responsibilities in the maintenance program.
An up-to-date network diagram that contains an accurate inventory of all
network boundaries is maintained.
This policy ensures the security of the organization’s environment, both
physical and digital. It aligns with best practices to prevent unauthorized
access, safeguard information, and maintain the availability of systems and
data.

name: Privacy
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy must be read, understood, and signed off on by all employees and
