contractors with access to personal information or privileged access to systems
containing or processing personal information.
This policy covers handling personal information collected by RRS or its
customers, including data that is stored, processed, or otherwise shared with
RRS, its systems, employees, and contractors. It covers collecting, processing,
storing, and deleting personal information. This policy also governs human
resource data as it must be protected in a similar fashion as personal data of
users and customers and all other personal information that may be collected,
processed, or stored by RRS.
Legal Considerations
RRS is headquartered in ___________. Most of its employees are located there,
with some located in _______. RRS’s customers are primarily located in
________with some in _______. This means that RRS has to consider a variety
of regional, national, and international laws within the context of its privacy
program. __________ have been used as the baseline for the privacy program at
RRS, adjusting the privacy policy program to the most stringent legal
requirements to which personal data processing is subject.
Data Controller and Data Processor
RRS’s business customers are the data controllers for most information
entered into the RRS web application, website, and supporting systems or
shared periodically with RRS employees to deliver services. This positions RRS
as the data processor for most information stored and processed by RRS. Some
pieces of information are collected directly by RRS to facilitate security, logging,
and application performance. These items include IP addresses and behavior
within the RRS platform. For these pieces of information, RRS acts as the data
controller and processor. Additionally, RRS employs a variety of technologies
and partners that periodically act as sub-processors. If users have any questions
or concerns about the processing and handling of their personal information,
they may communicate directly with the Privacy Officer.
Privacy Notice and Transparency
It is important ethically and legally to provide reasonable transparency to data
subjects concerning the processing and handling of their personal data. RRS
maintains an up-to-date privacy notice that is made available to all customers
and users of the RRS platform and services. Employees and contractors must
read this privacy notice. If errors or concerns are discovered, findings must be
shared with the Privacy Officer.
Privacy by Design
The concept of privacy by design must be applied to every new product,
project, or service and if a change of substance to a current product, project, or
service occurs. Privacy by design involves considering privacy at every project
stage: planning, design, development, testing, launch, maintenance, and end of
life.
In applying privacy by design, the following elements must be considered:
Types of Data Collected
The Purposes of Processing
Legal Basis of Processing
Data Residency and Cross-Border Transfer
Retention Time
Data Subject Rights
A privacy impact assessment and a threat risk assessment must be conducted
as part of the planning and design phases of the project. They must be
updated before launch to factor in changes in scope that occur throughout the
product development. Additionally, these assessments must be reviewed at
least annually or in the event of a significant change in scope, business use
case, architecture, or legal landscape.
Legal Basis of Processing
Below are the legal bases for RRS to collect personal information:
Users have given their consent for one or more specific purposes.
Provision of data is necessary for the performance of an agreement with
the user.
Processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation.
Processing is necessary for the legitimate interests pursued by the
controller or a third party.
Retention Time
Personal data is to be processed and stored for as long as required to fulfill the
purpose for which it is collected.
Therefore:
Personal data collected to perform a contract between RRS and a business
customer is retained until such contract has been entirely performed or
the customer asks for the data to be deleted.
Personal data collected for RRS’s legitimate interests shall be retained as
long as needed to fulfill such purposes.
RRS may be allowed to retain personal information for a more extended period
whenever the user has consented to such processing, as long as such consent
is not withdrawn. Furthermore, RRS may be obliged to retain personal data for a
more extended period whenever required to perform a legal obligation or upon
order of an authority.
Once the retention period expires, the user’s personal data will be securely
deleted.
Processing Requests and Inquiries from Data Owners
Withdraw their consent at any time. Users have the right to withdraw
consent after they have previously given their consent to processing their
personal data.
Object to processing of their data. Users have the right to object to the
processing of their data if the processing is carried out on a legal basis
other than consent.
Access their data. Users have the right to learn if RRS is processing their
data, obtain disclosure regarding certain aspects of the processing, and
obtain a copy of the data undergoing processing.
Verify and seek rectification. Users have the right to verify their data
accuracy and ask for it to be updated or corrected.
Restrict the processing of their data. Users have the right, under certain
circumstances, to restrict the processing of their data. In this case, RRS will
not process their data for any purpose other than storing it.
Have their personal data deleted or otherwise removed. Users have the
right, under certain circumstances, to obtain the erasure of their data from
RRS.
Receive their data and have it transferred to another controller. Users
have the right to receive their data in a structured, commonly used,
machine-readable format, and, if technically feasible, to have it transmitted
to another controller without any hindrance. This provision is applicable
provided that the data is processed by automated means and that the
processing is based on the user’s consent, on a contract that the user is
part of, or on pre-contractual obligations.
Lodge a complaint. Users have the right to bring a claim before their
competent data protection authority.
Process for Data Subject to Exercise These Rights
Any requests to exercise data subject rights can be directed to RRS through the
contact details provided in this document. These requests can be exercised
free of charge and will be addressed by RRS as early as possible and always
within one month.
Cookie Policy
The RRS website and RRS web application use cookies.
Users must be able to learn more about the use of cookies via a cookie notice
that must be available to the user.

name: Risk and Compliance Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy governs RRS’s risk management program. The following must read,
understand, and follow this policy: all employees and contractors on the
Security or Privacy Team, all employees that manage or direct projects or
departments within the organization, and all principal decision-makers.
This policy defines:
Key objectives of the risk management program
Risk management roles and responsibilities
RRS’s risk management methodology
Privacy and security by design
Accuracy and transparency
This policy specifies when to perform risk assessments of RRS equipment,
systems, and data systems. The policy also outlines who is responsible for this
ongoing process of discovering, identifying, correcting, and preventing
potential security issues. A strong Risk Management Policy helps RRS
determine and resolve security weaknesses to prevent them from being
exploited.
Key Objectives of the Risk Management Program
The following are the core objectives of RRS’s risk management program:
Ensure senior management has clear and accurate information about
potential threats to the organization, including cyber threats; legal
exposure; and threats to people, assets, revenue, the brand, the
organization, and its stakeholders.
Provide guidance to privacy and security personnel and company leadership
regarding budget and human resource requirements, priorities, and project
timelines in remediation and risk mitigation strategies.
Risk Management Roles and Responsibilities
Security Officer
The Security Officer is responsible for managing and administrating the risk
management program. This includes:
Managing and scheduling risk assessments
Communicating the risk management program throughout the
organization
Leading mitigation efforts
Security Team
The Security Team, led by the Security Officer, is collectively responsible for
ensuring that risk assessments are comprehensive, accurate, transparent, and
void of conflict. The team is responsible for ensuring that key perspectives and
inputs are at the table when assessing impact, threat, and risk and that
remediation efforts and resource requests are clearly and comprehensively
presented to senior management and key stakeholders.
Privacy Officer
The Privacy Officer is responsible for overseeing RRS’s compliance with privacy
laws and regulations. This role involves developing and implementing privacy
policies, conducting privacy impact assessments, ensuring data protection
practices, integrating with data breach incidents, providing staff training on
privacy matters, and serving as a point of contact for individuals regarding
privacy concerns and rights. The Privacy Officer will:
Act as the Data Protection Officer (DPO) if GDPR requires one or if the
organization determines that it is in its best interest to have one. GDPR
requires public organizations to appoint a DPO if core activities involve
processing personal data on a large scale or if they consistently process
highly sensitive data.
Work with regulators to help ensure compliance.
Ensure the training program covers appropriate privacy regulations and
requirements.
Keep up with changes in law and technology.
Develop, implement, and manage the privacy program.
Senior Management
Senior management must approve all actions determined from the results of
risk assessments, including decisions to accept, transfer, or mitigate risk. The
Security Team primarily runs the risk management process and makes sure the
results and findings of risk assessments are presented to and understood by
senior management. Senior management then approves remediation initiatives,
priorities, timelines, and budgets.
All Employees
Other employees within the organization may be called upon to assist in
assessing or mitigating risk. If this happens, the employee must participate and
support the risk initiative as required.
RRS’s Risk Management Methodology
RRS uses the Carbide Continuous Risk Methodology (CCRM), which has been
designed to provide guidance and structure to ensure that agile, high-growth
companies are able to efficiently and effectively manage information security,
data privacy and overall organizational risk on a continuous basis.
The methodology provides a framework for RRS to ensure that information
security, privacy, and risk management are integrated into every facet of
business decision-making as well as within project and product development
initiatives. The methodology also provides consideration for a variety of
common constraints and limitations that can challenge security, privacy, and
risk management initiatives within modern growth-focused organizations.
RRS while employing the CCRM, leverages independent third-party
assessments and penetration tests annually in conjunction with assessments
conducted by the Security Team to ensure that penetration tests, risk
assessments, and privacy impact assessments are comprehensive and to
ensure impartiality of the results.
When to Perform Risk Assessments
Risk assessments must be conducted at least annually. Additionally, when
significant changes at RRS occur, a risk assessment must be performed to
assess the impact those changes have on organizational risk. The following is a
list of events that would require engaging the risk management team (the
Security Team) to ensure an assessment is made:
Introduction of new projects and processes
External factors, such as regulatory, consumer pressure, environmental,
geopolitical, and economic stability
Changes in the scope of current products, processes, or projects
Any event that changes the scope of data being collected or processed
Significant change to the business model or a pivot
Leadership or management change that could affect risk appetite
Major changes to any critical systems and technology
Changes to or introductions of business relationships, vendors, and
partnerships
Mergers and acquisitions
Privacy and Security by Design
RRS applies the concepts of security and privacy by design to all stages of
conceptualization, design, development, deployment, and management of all
major projects. Privacy and security by design refer to considering privacy and
security at every level of all major projects. This ongoing consideration ensures
better design and planning compared to only reviewing privacy and security
considerations at the end of a project before it goes live.
Accuracy and Transparency
Addressing the possibility of fraudulent or inaccurate claims or activities in risk
assessments is another factor in successfully managing risk at RRS.
All individuals who participate in risk management must ensure that they act
ethically, honestly, and transparently. Ensuring that conflicting pressures or
incentives do not influence assessment results and outcomes is ultimately the
responsibility of the Security Officer. That said, everyone must play a part in
ensuring risk assessments are performed at RRS are mindful of:
Opportunities where fraud or inaccuracy could occur
Incentives or pressures to commit fraudulent acts or underplay risks to the
business
How fraudulent reports could be justified at various levels of management
If a concern arises, it must be brought to the Security Team or senior
management.
Compliance Audits
Access Control Audits
Access control audits are performed at RRS on a quarterly basis and are the
responsibility of the Security Officer, or an approved designated Security Team
member.
The results of access control audits are reviewed by at least two members of
the security team, and a summary of the findings will be provided to senior
management.
Access control audits will consist of the following:
Review the password security of all passwords to critical systems and
services employed at RRS.
Review the Multi-factor authentication (“MFA”) enforcement of all critical
systems and services employed at RRS.
Review employee access to ensure that role-based access control is
enforced on all RRS critical systems, data, and services.
Identify and flag any occurrences of non-compliance with role-based
access control, password security, and MFA enforcement.
Provide a summary report of the access control review to at least two
members of the security team and senior management.
Employee Onboarding, Termination, and Transfer
Audits
Employee onboarding, termination, and transfer audits are performed at RRS on
a basis no less than annually and are the responsibility of the Security Officer,
or an approved designated security team member. The recurrence of these
audits should depend on the rate at which new employees are onboarded,
employees are terminated, or transferred to different lines of the business, and
therefore recurrence should be decided by senior management.
The results of these audits are reviewed by at least two members of the
security team, and a summary of the findings will be provided to managers of
each team that has onboarded employees in the audit period. Employee
onboarding, termination, and transfer audits will consist of the following:
Employee Onboarding Audit
Employee onboarding audits are a review of the employee onboardings which
occurred since the last audit.
These audits consist of the following:
Review the policy sign-off of employees onboarded in the audit period
Review the completion of awareness training assigned to employees
onboarded in the audit period
Review the access granted to employees onboarded in the audit period to
make sure it meets the employee’s role
Review the confidentiality agreement, probationary period status, and any
other stipulations to the employee’s employment in the audit period to
ensure compliance with the employee’s contractual requirements
Provide a summary report of the employee onboarding audit to at least two
members of the security team and managers of each team that has
onboarded employees in the audit period.
Employee Termination Audit
Employee termination audits are a review of the employee terminations since
the last audit.
These audits consist of the following:
Review the accounts of employees terminated during the audit period to
ensure accounts are disabled
Review any other provisioned access of employees terminated during the
audit period to ensure access is revoked
Review exit interviews of employees terminated during the audit period to
ensure that exit interviews were conducted and equipment is returned,
and contractual obligations which survive termination are reinforced, such
as any confidentiality requirements, non-compete, and non-solicitation
clauses.
Review to ensure data loss prevention of data from terminated employees’
accounts and access is transferred to the employees’ manager or role
replacement.
Provide a summary report of the employee termination audit to at least
two members of the security team and managers of each team that has
terminated employees in the audit period.
Employee Transfer Audit
Employee transfer audits are a review of the employee transfers which
occurred since the last audit.
These audits consist of the following:
Review the policy sign-off of employees transferred in the audit period and
ensure that those employees have been required to read, acknowledge and
sign policies that apply to them.
Review the completion of awareness training assigned to employees
transferred in the audit period and ensure that those employees have
been assigned and have completed any awareness training which applies
to them.
Review the access control of transferred employees during the audit
period to ensure compliance to role-based access of the new role.
Review any other provisioned access of employees transferred during the
audit period to ensure access is revoked to systems and services which the
transferred employees no longer require.
Review to ensure data loss prevention of data from transferred employees’
accounts and access is transferred to the employees’ old manager or new
role replacement.
Provide a summary report of the employee transfer audit to at least two
members of the security team and managers of each team that has
transferred employees in the audit period.
Internal Compliance Audit
Internal compliance audits are a review of the internal control environment of
RRS compliance with the information security policies and controls selected as
part of RRS’s risk management and compliance.
Internal compliance audits are the responsibility of the security officer or an
approved designated security team member and must be conducted no less
than annually.
Internal compliance audits consist of the following:
Review and ensure RRS is in compliance with organizational information
security policies and selected controls.
Review and ensure RRS is in compliance with customer contractual
requirements and service level agreements.
Review and ensure RRS is in compliance with any applicable legal and
regulatory requirements.
Provide a summary report of the internal compliance audit to the Security
Team and senior management.
External Third-party Audit
External third-party audits are a review and testing exercise by a third-party
audit entity such as an auditing firm or regulatory body that validates RRS
information security is in compliance with a specific framework, contractual
obligation, or legal and regulatory requirement.
External third-party audits may be required as part of a contractual, legal, or
regulatory obligation. This type of audit may also be the voluntary mechanism
of good faith for RRS privacy and security commitments.
Managing and coordinating external third-party audits are the responsibility of
the Security Officer or a designated and approved member of the Security
Team.
The cadence and recurrence in which these audits are conducted are typically
annually or at the discretion of a customer contractual obligation or legal and
regulatory requirement.

name: Secure System Development
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy outlines RRS’s secure software development strategies and provides
an overview of the organization’s approach to developing software securely.
This policy is the basis for the governance, design, implementation, verification,
and operation of RRS’s software development life cycle (SDLC). This policy
provides direction and defines how to securely build software at RRS.
This policy is intended for all RRS employees and contractors who develop,
design, change, or contribute to software development. Everyone who has
access to RRS code and code repositories, development environments, or key
material, and everyone who makes changes to software intended for
production must read and follow this policy. Everyone with privileges must
develop, manage, and change software at RRS plays a role in ensuring that
software is developed securely and RRS achieves and maintains the highest
standards concerning software development from a security and quality
perspective.
This policy defines:
Secure software development objectives and strategies
Security by design
Standard technology and practices
Secure development and deployment
Verification and testing
Secure software development operations
Key management
This policy is based on the Open Web Application Security Project Software
Assurance Maturity Model version 2 (OWASP SAMM v2).
The OWASP SAMM v2 Overview
The OWASP SAMM v2 is a maturity model that companies and development
teams use globally to help assess, establish, and continuously improve secure
software development practices. RRS leverages the OWASP SAMM v2, OWASP
SAMM v2.0, to successfully implement and maintain its industry-accepted best
practices in secure software development.
Secure Software Development Objectives and
Strategies
Strategy and Metrics
RRS must determine what objectives and metrics to leverage to ensure its
software development security outcomes are met. Not all assets or projects are
equally important. Efforts in creating and measuring metrics for successful
outcomes must be allocated unevenly, where the most important assets or
projects receive the most security consideration, resources, and planning.
In preparing projects and securing software development assets, RRS must:
Identify metrics to be used to measure the success of projects and the
organization’s overall application security.
Develop and maintain a strategic roadmap for application security for the
organization.
Allocate and align security efforts proportionately to the asset’s value.
Establish a baseline or minimum standard for application security.
Communicate and hold software developers accountable for application
security through shared team and individual metrics.
Align team and individual application security metrics with the
organization’s objectives.
Policy and Compliance
This policy is instrumental to the governance and realization of compliance
objectives for RRS and must be followed. Compliance with this policy must be
monitored and measured to fulfill its purpose effectively.
RRS is committed to obtaining and maintaining compliance with the
compliance objectives defined in the Governance Policy.
RRS also commits to maintaining compliance with the following customer-
specific requirements for software development and application security:
Customer Requirements
To assist in obtaining and maintaining organizational compliance objectives, RRS
leverages OWASP SAMM v2 to implement and maintain its industry-accepted
best practices in secure software development.
Education and Guidance
RRS must maintain a robust training program to reinforce good practices,
correct poor practices, and provide role-specific training and guidance around
secure software development and deployment.
Resources must be available to all software developers for secure development
and deployment. Gaps must be identified and closed by educating personnel
on the software lifecycle, best practices, and their role in software
development and deployment.
A combination of in-house training and external third-party software
development and deployment training shall be provided to all software
developers and those in the developer team to ensure a high standard of
quality and security.
Security by Design
During the design and planning phases of software development projects at
RRS, security requirements are defined, potential threats are identified, and
supporting resources and infrastructure are prepared to facilitate the secure
development and deployment of high-quality software.
Threat Assessment
High-level threats to the organization and individual projects are assessed.
Informal and formal threat assessments must be conducted to make the best-
effort identification of credible threats to the organization and individual
project success during the design and planning phases. Threat assessments
conducted at RRS on the organization’s application security posture must
consider the following:
The risk profile of RRS based on known and unknown threats
The risk appetite of RRS based on leadership and stakeholder engagement
and risk tolerance
The likelihood and impact of threats
Maintaining a single source of truth for the organization of application
security and organizational risks, also known as a risk register as per the
Risk Management Policy
Keeping the risk register updated and accurate with periodic reviews and
assessments of threats and risks relating to application security as per the
Risk Management Policy
Security Requirements
Security requirements for software development projects at RRS must consider
the following:
The high-level functional requirements’ alignment with desired security
outcomes of the application
The minimum security requirements for application security
Any customer-specific requirements or applicable contractual obligations
related to application security
Security Architecture
Architecture design considers proactive security guidance in the software
design process at RRS. All components, libraries, tools, and resources used in
the architecture of the software must undergo scrutiny, monitoring, and review
from a security perspective throughout the lifecycle of the software. The
security of open, closed, and proprietary software, libraries, and components
used to support and deliver the application are monitored and managed via
automated tooling and manual review. The software design process must be
facilitated by and directed with secure by design practices and principles. RRS
must enforce the use of standardized technologies in the design and
development of software.
Standard Technology and Practices
Source Code Management
All source code written for RRS applications should be stored in a source code
manager.
RRS uses INPUT SYSTEM FOR SCM for source code management.
Documentation
Documentation for requirements and specifications should be developed as
needed and on a just-in-time basis for new software to be developed.
Automated Testing
RRS employs the use of automated testing. Application features should have
suitable automated tests written alongside the application code.
Continuous Integration
RRS uses continuous integration (CI) for testing application code automatically.
New code should not be released without first passing CI checks.
RRS uses INPUT SYSTEM FOR CI for continuous integration.
Separation of Deployment Environments
RRS uses separate deployment environments to ensure that production data
cannot be accessed by development environments, staging environments, and
unauthorized parties, both internal and external to the organization. Production
data should never be deployed to a non-production environment, and
appropriate access controls must be maintained to protect production data.
RRS maintains the following separate deployment environments:
Production for customer use; and
Non-production environments for activities such as testing, debugging,
quality assurance, and non-customer-facing activities.
Code Review
All RRS code should be reviewed by one or more other developers to help
assess code quality, architectural soundness, specification completion, and
security implications.
Secure Development and Deployment
Secure Build
RRS must employ a build process that:
Is repeatable and consistent;
Is optimized;
Is fully integrated into a widely accepted workflow; and
Prevents known defects from entering the production environment.
This build process should enable RRS to work more efficiently and effectively
and build a better, more secure product without being unsustainable to
development time and other resource costs.
Secure Deployment
RRS must employ a fully documented deployment process that:
Is fully automated;
Protects production secrets and key material; and
Incorporates automated security verification testing.
Exception Tracking
RRS must employ an exception tracking system to capture errors and
exceptions in production environments to help with issue analysis and repair.
RRS uses INPUT SYSTEM FOR EXCEPTIONS for exception tracking.
Automated Analysis, Linting, and Code Quality Reporting
RRS uses automated source code analysis tools to provide reports for code
quality, security vulnerability reporting, and various software development
metrics.
RRS uses the following automated analysis tools:
Automated analysis tool A
Automated analysis tool B
Automated analysis tool C
Verification and Testing
RRS must employ proper verification and testing practices to ensure the quality
and security of code and application environments are maintained at a
minimum baseline standard.
Architecture Assessment
Cloud environments that host applications at RRS must be held to the
standard of security and availability defined in the Cloud Management and
Availability Management policies. Regular assessment of these environments is
required to monitor and maintain compliance with this policy.
Requirements-Driven Testing
Requirements-driven testing determines whether implemented security
controls operate as expected and provide anticipated outcomes. RRS should
employ both requirements-driven testing and regression testing when
possible to find unexpected design flaws in addition to testing the security
controls for expected outcomes.
The following additional testing cases should be employed to maintain a high
standard for requirements-driven testing when applicable:
Security Fuzzing Tests
Business Logic Flaws Tests
Abuse Case Tests
Denial of Service Tests
Security Stress Tests
Security Testing
Security testing at RRS must focus on two key categories of vulnerabilities:
Those that are easy to detect and fix; and
Those that are more complex and harder to resolve.
The following security testing must be performed on all applicable software
projects and applications at RRS:
Implementing automated testing and tools that detect vulnerabilities in
the application and provide context and remediation assistance or, in some
cases, automated remediation; and
Having in-house and third-party experts perform manual reviews and
tests.
Secure Software Development Operations
Issue Tracking and Management
RRS tracks software issues in an issue tracking system. Issues may include
feature requests, bug reports, story tickets, development task lists, and the
issue tracking system should be accessible by developers, managers, and any
employees or contractors directly involved in the software development
lifecycle process.
Issue tracking and management must be used to help reduce the number of
new and recurring issues. Issues should influence the deployment process so
that software is deployed to production with the fewest number of issues
possible.
RRS uses INPUT SYSTEM FOR ISSUE TRACKING for tracking issues.
Issues resulting from incidents will be managed per the incident response plan
in the Incident Response for Security Team Policy.
Environment Hardening
Environments hosting and supporting applications, particularly production
applications, must apply patching and hardening measures to the following
standard:
Active monitoring of security configurations for nonconformance to
security baselines in production environments
Active monitoring of patch levels, statuses, and when scheduled patches
are applied
Formalizing the documentation process for security configuration and
patch management processes
Key Management
The purpose of encryption keys is to ensure the confidentiality of data at rest
and in transit. Data could include user and administrative credentials, customer
data, personal information of individuals, or the sensitive, proprietary data
belonging to RRS and its partners.
Key Types
Controls and procedures must be in place to protect and manage encryption
keys. RRS manages the following encryption keys:
INPUT KEY TYPES
Key Management System
RRS uses the following key management system to generate, distribute, and
manage encryption keys:
INPUT KEY MANAGEMENT SYSTEMS
Controls for Encryption Keys
The encryption keys listed in use at RRS have the following measures in place to
control encryption keys:
INPUT KEY CONTROLS
Lifecycle of Encryption Keys
Cryptographic key material will have a lifespan of no longer than one year from
the time it is generated.
Cryptographic keys must be replaced at the end of their lifespan and must be
monitored for deprecation.
Cryptographic key deprecation can occur due to a predefined lifespan set by
the industry or when the encryption key has been determined to no longer be
secure due to the advancement of “cracking” by brute force or other
technological and mathematical advancements.
Change Management
RRS must ensure the following change management requirements are met:
Ensure the asset/product owner authorizes all changes to software and
software packages.
Create methods for monitoring data for unauthorized changes.
Identify owners for specific configuration items.
Authorize owners of particular software products at RRS to determine the
value and importance of specific configuration items.
Back up all data and perform needed maintenance before installing new
software and updating current software.
Ensure all changes can be reverted in the case that they are unsuccessful.
Record all changes.
Adopt a standard naming and numbering convention for identifying all
changes.

name: Third-Party Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
This policy governs RRS’s third-party vendor management activities. The
following must read, understand, and follow this policy: all employees that
manage or own relationships with vendors or are responsible for third-party
vendor risk management.
Third-party vendors are defined as any third-party service provider, technology
provider, or contractor not owned and entirely controlled by RRS but is
contracted or otherwise utilized in business operations by RRS. Third-party
vendors can pose substantial risk to the business and must be managed
accordingly.
This policy defines:
Key objectives of the vendor management
Vendor management roles and responsibilities
RRS’s third-party vendor risk management methodology
Third-party vendor risk management
This policy specifies when to perform risk assessments of RRS third-party
vendors. The policy also outlines who is responsible for this ongoing process of
assessing third-party vendor risk and working with third-party vendors to
manage it. A robust Third-Party Management Policy helps RRS identify,
categorize, and manage third-party vendor relationships, risk, and resolve any
issues identified with third-party vendor applications, products, and services
throughout the lifecycle of the business relationship.
Key Objectives of Vendor Management
The following are the core objectives of RRS’s vendor management activities:
Ensure that third-party vendors, service providers, SaaS, or any other third
party who accesses RRS information and systems have been appropriately
vetted and contracted and is appropriately managed.
Ensure that senior management has clear and accurate information about
potential threats to the organization, including cyber threats; legal
exposure; and threats to people, assets, revenue, the brand, the
organization, and its stakeholders as a result of the relationship with the
third-party vendor.
To ensure the availability, confidentiality, and integrity of the data disclosed
to third-party vendors, the software and services provided by the third-
party vendor, and ultimately the risk introduced to RRS as a result of the
relationship with the vendor is measured and managed.
Vendor Management Roles and Responsibilities
Security Officer
The Security Officer is responsible for managing and administrating the vendor
management program. This includes:
Managing and scheduling risk assessments of third-party vendors
Communicating the vendor management program to appropriate teams
and individuals
Keeping senior management informed of potential threats to RRS,
including cyber threats; legal exposure; and threats to people, assets,
revenue, the brand, the organization, and its stakeholders as a result of the
relationship with the third-party vendor.
Acquiring and reviewing third-party vendor independent audit reports,
penetration tests, and relevant details about security and privacy, and
ensuring senior management is aware of new risks introduced and residual
risks sustained by the third-party vendor on a regular basis.
Security Team
The Security Team, led by the Security Officer, is collectively responsible for
ensuring that third-party vendor risk assessments are comprehensive, accurate,
transparent, and void of conflict. The team is responsible for ensuring that key
perspectives and inputs are at the table when assessing impact, threat, and risk
and that third-party vendor risk is clearly and comprehensively presented to
senior management and key stakeholders.
Privacy Officer
Companies that store or process personal information or otherwise have
privacy compliance requirements should have a person assigned as the owner
of the privacy program in the organization. The Privacy Officer is responsible for
ensuring that privacy considerations are factored into third-party vendor risk
assessments.
Senior Management
Senior management must approve all procurement of third-party vendors
products and services, as well as including decisions relating to what level of
risk is acceptable when procuring new products and services. The Security Team
primarily runs the vendor risk management process and makes sure the results
and findings of vendor risk assessments are presented to and understood by
senior management. Senior management then makes decisions about whether
to retain and accept the third-party vendor risk, to request remediation
initiatives, priorities, and timelines from the third-party vendor, or to
discontinue the business relationship with the third-party vendor.
All Employees
Specific employees and managers within the organization may be called upon
to assist in assessing and managing third-party vendor risk. If this happens, the
employee must participate and support the initiative as required.
All employees must agree only to use approved third-party vendors where RRS
has an approved and vetted product or service relationship. No employee will
use, download, or install unapproved or unvetted tools, services, or products
without authorization from the Security Team.
All employees must make a formal request to the Security Team for any
exceptions to this policy. Exceptions will be authorized on a case-by-case basis.
When to Perform Vendor Risk Assessments
Vendor risk assessments must be conducted at least annually. Additionally,
when significant changes to the third-party vendor relationship occur, a vendor
risk assessment must be performed to assess the impact those changes have
on organizational risk. The following is a list of events that would require
engaging the vendor risk management team (the Security Team) to ensure an
assessment is made:
When procuring new products or services from a new or existing third-
party vendor.
Changes in the scope of current products, services, or relationship with the
third-party vendor
Any event that changes the scope of data being collected or processed by
the third-party vendor
Significant change to the business model or a pivot
Major changes to the third-party vendor products or services
Mergers and acquisitions on either party in the third-party vendor
relationship
Using third-party vendor risk assessment results to inform and guide
decision-makers on the protection of PHI/ePHI and PI/PII when necessary
Third-Party Vendor Risk Management
Third-party vendor risk assessments must be conducted before procurement
of products or services of a third-party vendor, whenever the scope or use case
of a third-party vendor changes, and at least on an annual basis.
The risk management methodology includes special considerations for third
parties. The following high-level steps must be followed in the selection,
activation, management, and termination of third-party vendors:
1. Define the Scope of third-party
Define the use cases for the third party to determine what information or
systems the third party may access, as well as which employees, contractors,
customers, or other stakeholders will interact or interface with the third party.
Define an owner within RRS to be assigned the responsibility of managing the
third party throughout the engagement lifecycle.
2. Assess risk score for third-party
In the same way that other risks to the business are assessed, special
consideration for risks introduced by a third party must be assessed,
understood, and communicated to senior management. Once risks are
identified, appropriate mitigation measures must be documented,
implemented, and managed throughout the engagement lifecycle.
3. Conduct third-party vendor assessment
Based on the use case and scope defined in Step 1 and the risks identified in
Step 2, an assessment of the vendor must be conducted. Special care must be
taken to ensure the vendor has implemented and maintains appropriate
controls in order to protect RRS from threats to confidentiality, integrity,
availability, privacy, and other legal obligations. Established vendors may have
security and/or privacy reports to facilitate this process. They may have third-
party certifications or attestations that can be leveraged as well. These reports
must be reviewed and approved by RRS’s Security Team before the vendor can
be activated. In the event that the vendor does not have these resources
available for assessment, a security questionnaire appropriate to the use case
must be filled out by the vendor and reviewed by the Security Team. Depending
on the scope and risks identified in the first two steps, this may require
additional discussion, interviews, or other assessments by the Security Team to
ensure the vendor follows appropriate security and privacy practices for the
defined scope and use case.
4. Establish an appropriate documented relationship
A contracted relationship must be established with the vendor before
commencing the engagement. The following must be considered within the
contracted relationship:
The vendor must contractually commit to maintaining an information
security program that aligns with the security compliance and risk
tolerances defined by RRS and relative to the scope and risk of the
engagement.
The vendor must contractually commit to compliance with all applicable
privacy and security regulations, standards, and requirements identified
within the scope of the vendor use case.
5. Establish configuration or process baseline
Care must be taken to ensure that risks identified in the risk assessment are
mitigated by establishing configuration and/or process requirements to be
applied to the engagement. For technology vendors such as SaaS vendors, the
Security Team must ensure that the vendor owner conducts appropriate
research in order to document the scope of appropriate use, configuration
requirements, and access control.
6. Establish an appropriate assessment schedule
The above five steps must be reviewed annually to ensure that the scope, risk
profile, or threat landscape has not changed and to ensure the vendor use case
and compliance requirements are met.

name: Vulnerability and Patch Management
content:
Introduction and Scope
Vulnerability and Patch Management includes the activities, tools, and
strategies that are employed to identify, monitor, report, and resolve
vulnerabilities in systems and software. Auditing includes the internal audit of
controls on a regular cadence to ensure compliance with RRS information
security policies. This policy defines the requirements of RRS, governing what
activities, tools, and strategies are to be leveraged within the vulnerability
management program and what audit activities are performed, and to what
degree they are performed.
This policy is required to be followed by individuals responsible for running the
vulnerability management program, supporting activities, internal audit
activities, and managing or coordinating external third-party audits.
The vulnerability management for RRS focuses on scanning of its web
application environment and public-facing website. These environments are
critical to RRS from the perspective of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
The auditing policy RRS focuses on the following audit activities:
Access control audits
Employee onboarding, termination, and transfer audits
Internal RRS compliance audits of privacy and security
External third-party audits
Penetration Tests
Vulnerability Assessment Process
A documented vulnerability management process and appropriate tools for
conducting vulnerability assessments of identified critical systems must be
followed for all systems managed within the scope of this policy.
Vulnerability Scanning
RRS conducts monthly vulnerability scans using tools approved by the Security
Team to identify improper configurations, known vulnerabilities, unpatched
systems, and other potential vulnerabilities.
RRS conducts external penetration testing annually using a third-party provider.
Penetration Tests
Penetration Tests are a test of the security of an application, network, or
system performed by an approved external third-party using tools and
techniques employed by a malicious actor to simulate a real attack.
Penetration tests can be useful as a pressure-test, a security assessment, a
validation by a third party, and a way to discover previously unknown
vulnerabilities.
Managing and coordinating penetration tests are the responsibility of the
Security Officer or a designated and approved member of the Security Team.
Penetration testers performing penetration tests on RRS applications,
networks, or environments shall have appropriate security credentials,
experience, and knowledge applicable to the testing environment.
Appropriate certifications include:
General Penetration Testing Certifications
EC-Council Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH)
EC-Council Licensed Penetration Tester (LPT) Master
CompTIA PenTest+
Global Information Assurance Certification (GIAC) Penetration Tester
(GPEN)
Global Information Assurance Certification (GIAC) Exploit Researcher and
Advanced Penetration Tester (GXPN)
Offensive Security Certified Professional (OSCP)
Offensive Security Certified Expert (OSCE)
Web Application Penetration Testing Certifications
Offensive Security Web Expert (OSWE)
Global Information Assurance Certification (GIAC) Web Application
Penetration Tester (GWAPT)
Penetration Tests should be conducted no less than annually or when there are
significant changes to applications, environments, or network infrastructure
under the responsibility of RRS.
Vulnerability Resolution
Prioritization of the resolution of vulnerabilities is necessary when a significant
number of vulnerabilities are discovered.
Priority for the resolution of vulnerabilities will be assigned based on the level
of criticality and the degree of risk and exposure the vulnerability could create.
For each monitored system, a remediation process must be developed that
clarifies remediation responsibilities and sets a baseline for response times and
remediation times.
